title: K-Cup Holder Ladder
summary: A slim leaning ladder rack that holds coffee pods between evenly spaced rungs, tilted flat against the wall behind it.
description:
This is a small, fast build with one measurement worth getting right: rung spacing. The plan's own builder found 1-5/8" the right gap to hold a standard K-Cup pod snugly without it falling through, with 1-3/4" as an acceptable second choice — anything looser and the pods slide down and jumble together at the bottom. Mark every rung location before you nail a single one on, rather than spacing them by eye as you go, since a drifting gap becomes obvious the moment pods are loaded in.

The top and bottom rail angles are both 10°, not the more common 15° — worth double-checking on your saw before cutting, since it's an easy mix-up. Squaring off the top back corner is optional but worth doing if this will lean directly against a wall; it lets the ladder rest flush instead of pivoting on a single point of contact.

tools.note: For optionally squaring off the top back corner.
Set to 10°, not the more common 15°, for the rail angles.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 10° off square, ends parallel, long point to short point measurement.

steps.title: Cut the side rails
steps.body: Cut the two side rails with both ends at 10° off square — not the more common 15°, worth double-checking on your saw. The angles should be parallel to each other, long point to short point. If this will lean directly against a wall, you can optionally square off the top back corner with a jigsaw so it rests flush instead of pivoting on one point.

steps.title: Mark and attach the rungs
steps.body: Measure and mark out all five rung locations on both rails before nailing anything — a 1-5/8" gap between rungs holds a standard K-Cup pod snugly (1-3/4" also works). Glue and nail each rung in place with 1-1/4" brad nails.

images.alt: Small leaning wooden ladder rack for holding coffee pods
